title: Topology Declarations, Policies, and Migration · Program Defense
summary: A release board compares near-miss Go and RabbitMQ decisions for topology declarations, policies, and migration and accepts only bounded ownership, causal evidence, and an explicit live-broker transfer gate. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a versioned Go topology ownership manifest. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Assign ownership for exchanges, queues, bindings, policies, migrations, rollback, and deletion across application and platform teams; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Make QueueDeclare and ExchangeDeclare calls equivalent in type, durability, exclusivity, auto-delete, and immutable arguments and classify precondition errors.
Choose durable, exclusive server-named, auto-delete, and queue-TTL lifecycles from responsibility and race evidence; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Topology Migration
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz Go messaging team handles case 7963 during consumer shutdown drill with one blocked handler goroutine. Build deterministic Go evidence to protect declaration equivalence and route mutable changes through policy or migration; reject stale connection and channel generations; then fail the canary and prove older Go consumers remain compatible. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Migrate incompatible topology with versioned names, dual bindings, drain evidence, producer and consumer ordering, and rollback; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

Evidence target: RabbitMQ and Go Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Topology Migration.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for migrate incompatible topology with versioned names, dual bindings, drain evidence, producer and consumer ordering, and rollback; in go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence. For RabbitMQ and Go Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Topology Migration, keep Topology Migration connected to executable Go messaging decision evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Topology Migration
steps.instruction:
Write the quiz evidence note for Topology Migration: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A quiz Go messaging team handles case 8568 during race-enabled integration test with duplicate delivery. Build deterministic Go evidence to protect declaration equivalence and route mutable changes through policy or migration; allow exactly one terminal acknowledgement owner; then lose a quorum member and recompute majority availability. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. In Go, retrying a QueueDeclare precondition error cannot mutate the existing queue.
